persons.arguments_against: Counter-hypothesis: Chapman is a common surname (41 staff officials, 23 biographies), the bio carries only initials 'T. H.', the deterministic honour check FAILS, and edition co-occurrence is not met, so name and the mechanical scores alone would be weak. This fails because the staff stint and the bio trace the IDENTICAL multi-rung Ceylon Public Works ladder in the same order over 25+ years: District Engineer (bio 1888 / staff 1890) -> Provincial Engineer (bio 'ag. prov. engrnr., E. Prov', 1904 / staff 1906-09) -> Extra Assistant Director of Public Works (bio 'extra astt. D.P.W., 1909' / staff 1910-12) -> Assistant Director (bio 'astt. D.P.W., 1912' / staff 1913) -> Director of Public Works (bio 'D.P.W., Aug., 1913' / staff Director 1914-22). The terminal post is the singular Directorship. No second T. H. Chapman could climb this exact specific sequence in lockstep.

evidence_cited.fact: T. H. Chapman | Extra Assistant Director of Public Works
T. H. Chapman | Assistant Director of Public Works
T. H. Chapman | Director of Public Works

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second T. H. Chapman in the Ceylon Public Works Department following the same Provincial Engineer -> Director progression, or a chronology conflict at the District Engineer entry.

notes: Common surname and two-initial bio are decisively offset by a detailed, ordered multi-office career match through the Ceylon PWD ranks ending in the singular Directorship of Public Works. Deterministic honour/edition checks add nothing, but the career-shape continuity is overwhelming.
